pattern: One iterative post-order pass computes subtree sizes; a node is good exactly when all of its children's subtree sizes match.
hints: Root the tree at node 0 and think about what 'children's subtrees have the same size' means in terms of sizes you can compute bottom-up.
Do a post-order traversal (iterative to avoid recursion limits) computing subtree size for every node, then compare the sizes of each node's direct children.

Build an adjacency list, get a traversal order via a stack from the root, accumulate size[node] += size[child] walking the order in reverse, then for each node collect {size[c] for c in children} and count it as good when that set has at most one distinct value.

edge_inputs.description: n=2, single edge, both nodes trivially good
star graph, every leaf subtree size 1
one internal node with two unequal-size children subtrees
path graph (chain) - every node has at most one child
root with three children of different subtree sizes (none good at root)
wide tree, moderate size, mixed branching
deep skew where one branch is a long chain and the other a single leaf (root not good)

stress.description: random tree, 5000 nodes via random-parent generation
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, size)
    edges = []
    for i in range(1, n):
        p = rng.randrange(0, i)
        edges.append([p, i])
    return [edges]

stress.description: random bushy tree with capped branching factor, 20000 nodes
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, size)
    edges = []
    window = 6
    for i in range(1, n):
        lo = max(0, i - window)
        p = rng.randrange(lo, i)
        edges.append([p, i])
    return [edges]
stress.note: keeps recent-parent window so the tree stays bushy rather than a long chain
